summary:
The Battle of Grunwald (July 15, 1410 AD), known in German as the Battle of Tannenberg, was one of the largest battles in medieval European history and the decisive defeat of the [[event:3013|Teutonic Knights]] by a Polish-Lithuanian-Ruthenian coalition under King Władysław II Jagiełło of Poland and Grand Duke Vytautas of Lithuania, permanently crippling the military power of the Teutonic Order and securing Polish-Lithuanian supremacy over the Baltic region. The battle involved an estimated 30,000–40,000 combatants on the Polish side and perhaps 20,000–30,000 Teutonic Knights and their allies — the Grand Master Ulrich von Jungingen was killed on the field, along with most of his senior commanders.

The battle began when the Teutonic Knights attempted to provoke the Polish-Lithuanian forces by sending heralds with two empty scabbards (a ritual humiliation suggesting the Poles were too cowardly to fight). The subsequent engagement lasted much of the day; the decisive moment came when apparent Lithuanian flight drew Teutonic cavalry in pursuit, leaving the Polish flanks free to envelop the Knights' main formation. The victory did not immediately destroy the Teutonic State — the Order retained its Prussian territories for over a century — but it permanently ended its military expansion and offensive capability. The Battle of Grunwald became one of the defining moments in Polish national consciousness: the 500th anniversary in 1910 was a major patriotic event, and the battle has been celebrated in art, literature, and national mythology across Poland, Lithuania, and Ukraine as a symbol of Slavic resistance to Germanic aggression.
